content.full_text:
" 
.. 
..... 
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Aleshire & Wynder, LLP 
Anthony R. Taylor 
City Attorney 
City of Banning 
18881 Von Kannan Avenue, Suite 1700 
Irvine, CA 92612 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-027 
Dear Mr. Taylor: 
March to, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Banning City Council Mayor Art 
Welch, Mayor Pro Tern George Moyer, and Councilmember Ed Miller refarding their duties under 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, may Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller 
participate in decisions to resolve unpaid water utility fees between the City and a home owner's 
association in which all three city council members own residences? 
CONCLUSION 
Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller may participate in 
governmental decisions to resolve a water services issue between the City and the housing 
development Home Owner's Association in which they all own residences because the public 
generally exception applies. 
FACTS 
Your office represents the City of Banning as City Attorney. Within the City there is a 
residential senior living development called the Sun Lakes Country Club that is managed by the 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-027 
Page No. 2 
Sun Lakes Home Owners Association (HOA). The Sun Lakes Country Club development 
("Development") requires that at least one member of each household be 55 years or older. All 
other members of each household must be at least 45 years of age. The HOA is a nonprofit 
corporation and operates common amenities for all of its residents such as tennis courts and 
clubhouses within the development. By owning real property within the development, property 
owners are automatically members of the HOA. Members are not compensated in any way, but 
must pay a monthly homeowner's assessment. 
There are 3,327 residential real properties within the development. This means that there are 
3,327 households within the development. There are 3,327 members of the HOA. According to the 
most recent Southern California Association of Governments Profile of the City of Banning report 
(issued in May 2015) there are approximately 12,149 households in the City of Banning. Mayor 
Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller are residents and each own one 
residence within the development; therefore, they are members of the HOA. 
The City operates a water utility that provides water service to the development, including 
the HOA operated facilities. Four water meters, relating to water service provided to the HOA 
operated facilities, have underreported water usage over a number of years. These water meters 
relate to water usage at buildings such as the clubhouses and for irrigation purposes. Because of the 
underreported water usage, the HOA owes the City additional unpaid water utility fees, but the 
HOA disputes the actual amount owed. If the HOA pays the full amount demanded and transfers 
those costs to its members, the estimated total cost for each member/household is approximately 
$70.00. At this time, it is not clear if the HOA will transfer any of those costs to its members. 
If the HOA pays any amount, whether the full amount or otherwise, without transferring 
those costs to its members, it is likely that the HOA will pay out of its fund balance. Currently, the 
dispute is going through the administrative process; however, failing an agreeable resolution, the 
City could consider all of its options, including potential litigation. Resolution of this dispute may 
include an agreement between the City and HOA. You believe that the impact of any decision 
would apply equally to all the real properties within and residents of the development. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in 
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial 
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family on one or more of the official's economic interests. The financial interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect2 
investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
2 "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the 
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust 
in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a IO­
percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.) 


File No. A-16-027 
Page No. 3 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending 
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to 
the public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred 
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or 
gifts aggregating [$460]3 or more in value provided to, received by, or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. 
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental 
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her 
immediate family. 
Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller ("councilmembers") all 
have real property interests worth $2,000 or more located within the jurisdiction as they each own a 
residence in the jurisdiction and all are dues paying members of the HOA that represents their 
homeownership interests. 
The councilmembers also have an interest in their personal finances that could possibly be 
affected by the water service decisions because the costs resulting from the decisions could be 
passed down to the council members as members of the HOA. 
Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. A financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 
18701.) A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official 
or the official's agency. 
In this case the HOA is the subject of the decision, and the HOA is comprised of the 
properties within the Development, therefore the councilmembers' interests are explicitly involved 
in the decisions. Thus, we presume the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. 
3 The amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the Commission 
to equal the same amount detennined by the Commission pursuant to Section 89503(0. The quoted provision above has 
been modified with the current figure. 


FileNo. A-16-027 
Page No. 4 
The councilmembers' financial interests in their personal finances are not a named party in 
or the subject of the decisions. Thus, the applicable foreseeability standard for this financial interest 
is set forth in Regulation 18701(b) is as follows: "A financial effect need not be likely to be 
considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." Here because the 
decisions could result in costs being passed down to the council members there is a realistic 
possibility that there will be a financial effect upon the councilmembers' personal finances and 
therefore the effect is reasonable foreseeable. 
Materiality 
Real Property: The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a 
parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than a leasehold interest, is 
material whenever the governmental decision: 
"Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees or assessments that apply to the parcel." 
(Re,gulation 18702.2.) 
Because the decisions before the Banning City Council involve water fees that would be 
assessed to the HOA there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon the 
councilmembers' financial interest in their real property. 
Personal Finances: A financial effect on an official's personal finances is material if the 
official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss 
from the decision. In this case, it is not clear how the decisions will affect the property owners in 
development, though the affect could possibly be a $70.00 cost to each household. The cost could 
be greater iflitigation is involved. Therefore, there is a measurable loss that could result from the 
decisions and there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon each of the 
council members' personal finances.4 
Public Generally 
If a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon an official's financial interests is 
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, there is no conflict under the Act. 
(Regulation 18700 (d)(3).) 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interest is 
indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant 
segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique 
compared to the effect on the significant segment." (Regulation 18703.) 
4 Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material 
if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. (Regulation 18702(b).) 


Regulation 18703 provides: 
"(b) A significant segment of the public is at least 25 percent of: 
File No. A-16-027 
Page No. 5 
"(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property 
within the official's jurisdiction; or 
"(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction." 
According to the most recent Southern California Association of Governments Profile of the 
City of Banning report (issued in May 2015) there are approximately 12,149 households in the City 
of Banning. There are 3,327 households within the development. Thus the development accounts 
for approximately 27.38% of the total households within the City. This constitutes a significant 
segment of the public. 
Regulation 18703(c) states "A unique effect on a public official's financial interest includes 
a disproportionate effect on: 
"(1} The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity .... 
"(3} An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the 
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or properties that is 
substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
"(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the 
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a 
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage." 
" 
"(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family." 
You state, the impact of any decision would apply equally to all the real properties within 
the development. The effect on the councilmembers' financial interests will be the same as it will be 
on all of the properties in the significant segment. 
Consequently, the public generally exception will apply and there would be no conflict of 
interest under the Act for City Council Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember 
Miller. 
Your facts indicate that it is too early to detennine if a settlement agreement or contract will 
result from these governmental decisions. For example you state that these decisions could result in 
litigation, which would not involve a contract. If a contract is involved in these governmental 
decisions, including any settlement agreement, please seek additional assistance under Government 
Code Section 1090. 


File No. A-16-027 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Commission Counsel 
Legal Division

association in which all three city council members own residences?

Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller may participate in 
governmental decisions to resolve a water services issue between the City and the housing 
development Home Owner's Association in which they all own residences because the public 
generally exception applies.

percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the Act, may Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller 
participate in decisions to resolve unpaid water utility fees between the City and a home owner's 
association in which all three city council members own residences?

CONCLUSION: Mayor Welch, Mayor Pro Tern Moyer, and Councilmember Miller may participate in 
governmental decisions to resolve a water services issue between the City and the housing 
development Home Owner's Association in which they all own residences because the public 
generally exception applies.
